behaviors.description: Rows with missing required fields are rejected and appear in the dead-letter queue with the original row number.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: failure is at module handoff (CSV row index + schema validator + DLQ writer), not just one function. REAL-INFRA: no real OS/network/subprocess is required if DLQ is an in-process sink/fake. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: not applicable. REJECTION-GATE: System was considered, but behavior text only requires rows to appear in DLQ with row number; it does not require real queue transport semantics.
behaviors.test_description: Wire parser, validator, and DLQ publisher together with an in-memory DLQ test double. Feed CSV rows with missing required fields and assert rejected records include exact original row numbers and are absent from the valid-output stream.

behaviors.description: The transformation correctly converts date strings in multiple formats (ISO 8601, US, European) to a single canonical format.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: behavior is pure parsing/normalization logic within transformation code. REAL-INFRA: no OS/network/subprocess needed. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered, but behavior text is limited to date-format conversion correctness, which is a single-function/object boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Table-driven unit test over representative and edge-case date strings for ISO 8601, US, and European formats; assert canonical output and invalid-date error handling.

behaviors.description: A CSV file with 500,000 rows completes processing within the configured timeout without running out of memory.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: timeout and memory-exhaustion are runtime/OS-level behaviors; in-process mocks can hide allocator/GC/streaming issues. MOCK-HIDE: small synthetic or mocked streams may pass while real large-file processing OOMs or breaches timeout. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered, but behavior explicitly requires timeout and memory guarantees under large-load execution.
behaviors.test_description: Run the real pipeline process against a generated 500k-row CSV and real runtime limits (configured timeout, memory observation). Assert completion before timeout and peak memory stays below threshold; fail on OOM/kill/timeout.

behaviors.description: When the S3 bucket returns a transient 503 error, the pipeline retries with exponential backoff rather than failing the entire batch.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: retry/backoff behavior is often implemented in real HTTP client/SDK middleware; pure mocks can bypass it. MOCK-HIDE: stubbing high-level client methods can falsely pass while real request path fails to retry correctly. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered, but behavior text references actual S3 503 retry/backoff semantics, best validated via real HTTP interaction (e.g., S3 emulator or controllable HTTP endpoint).
behaviors.test_description: Execute pipeline against an S3-compatible endpoint/test server that returns 503 for initial attempts then success. Measure attempt timing to verify exponential backoff and assert batch succeeds without full failure.

behaviors.description: Rows successfully written to PostgreSQL are not duplicated if the pipeline is restarted mid-batch.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: failure occurs at interaction boundary between checkpoint/progress tracking and DB writes. REAL-INFRA: requires a real/local PostgreSQL instance (local DB fits Integration) but not real external network or OS subprocess orchestration. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) no mandatory OS/network/subprocess behavior beyond local module+DB wiring, (b) no AI output dependency, (c) no multi-agentic chain. REJECTION-GATE: System was considered, but behavior only requires restart semantics and dedup correctness, which can be validated by two controlled runs against the same DB state.
behaviors.test_description: Using local PostgreSQL, run batch partially, simulate mid-batch restart by reinitializing pipeline with persisted state, then continue processing same input. Assert final table has exactly-once rows (no duplicates) and expected row count.
